chapters.title: It’s not just about looks
chapters.text:
Our clothing choices impact more than our style; they affect the environment and contribute to climate change. Many of us change outfits frequently without considering disposal or care, rarely repairing or altering clothes. We often have more than we need, failing to appreciate their beauty.

This insatiable desire for new clothes fuels a market rife with environmental and labor issues, tragically demonstrated by Bangladesh's 2013 Rana Plaza building collapse. To address these challenges, we must understand the importance of curbing excessive purchases and learn responsible methods for discarding, repairing, and reusing textiles. The more we know about the fabrics we use, the better it is for the global community.

Try this

• Invest in care products to maintain shoes and outerwear.
• Take inventory of your wardrobe; recycle or donate unused items.
• Maintain frequently worn clothes by repairing them, such as sewing new buttons.
• Replace impulse buying habits with healthier activities, like cooking or playing an instrument.

Read it till the end?

chapters.title: You’re in charge of the fabrics you wear
chapters.text:
Clothes connect us all. Even if we're not fashion lovers, we all wear clothes. Every day, we choose what to wear — some good, some not. The global fashion industry produces an astounding 100 billion pieces of clothing annually, often from unknown sources and exploitative systems, to meet our seemingly endless demand.

That's an unbelievable amount, isn't it? Shops and warehouses overflow with clothing, yet much goes unsold. It's impractical to keep buying and discarding, thinking it all disappears. Instead, we must consume more thoughtfully, mend what we have, and reuse them.

We must form emotional connections with our purchases, choosing items that reflect our feelings more than our reasoning.

It's hard to deny the damage mass production, consumption, and waste do to our planet and society. The constant noise from social media and ads makes it nearly impossible to choose wisely — endless trends, influencer suggestions, and compelling marketing tricks exist.

And we usually keep buying into them, causing more stuff to spread like a hedge around us. Everything we've ever thrown away is still here, either enhancing someone else's life or polluting a landfill. It's all because we often overlook maintenance — the crucial process of balancing consumption and waste.

The time has come to revalue old wisdoms and techniques and inject them with a fresh perspective, by turning oppression into opportunity. ~ Orsola de Castro

To fight wasteful discarding, we must learn to retain our things. Everything around us encourages dumping, so we should practice keeping instead. Maintaining and caring should be part of everything we do, even if it means stepping out of our comfort zones. Repairing items is doable; while it might not always be convenient, it's a small step that can lead to considerable changes. Whether we've splurged on a designer shirt or bought it from a chain store, the same rule applies: if it breaks, mend it. This way benefits the shirt, our community, the planet, and the people who make our clothes.

Did you know? The second-hand apparel market is booming. Experts predict it will nearly double to $351 billion by 2027 from its current value.

chapters.title: Daring to change
chapters.text:
Humans now face one of their biggest survival challenges yet: climate change. As we move toward a less polluting society, we'll need to adapt our behavior to meet these changing needs, which will require forming new habits.

But this doesn't mean giving up the joy and excitement of shopping for clothes. Instead, it’s about finding a balance between thinking and doing. It's about rediscovering the satisfaction of personally mending things and the sense of purpose that comes from valuing our possessions. Above all, this process is about rethinking what's convenient and realizing that making changes is much simpler than initially thought. It will take dedication and practical thinking, but all it needs is a series of manageable actions that we can easily fit into our daily lives.

For instance, to fix a tear in a boro cloth (intricate patchwork made from kimono leftovers) or any garment, follow these steps:

• Begin by pinning a piece of fabric to the front and rear of the tear. Check that the fabric covers and extends beyond the rip to make the patches stick to the material surrounding the tear.
• Use an embroidery needle and white thread to make running stitches over the entire patch. Tie it to the cloth and the backing patch from the inside.

Mending doesn't mean we can't afford to buy new things; it just means we can't afford to throw things away.

Mending techniques have a natural beauty that requires precision and creativity; this aspect of mending should appeal to us. Each item we restore is uniquely beautiful, showcasing our skill, innovation, and time. Putting our mending on display is a statement of purpose, similar to taking care of other repairs in our lives.

chapters.title: Repairing is the new black
chapters.text:
Hand-washing clothes requires enormous strength and takes a lot of time. It's no wonder that women created many strategies to delay washing until utterly necessary. Spot cleaning with a brush or spray was the go-to fix for small messes. Today, throwing a heap of laundry in the washing machine is much more convenient than hand-washing every stain that appears during the week.

But there are still several techniques that can replace machine washing, such as:

Steaming

• Turn on the hot water, shut the shower curtain or door, and hang your clothes as close to the tap as possible without getting them wet.
• Close the bathroom door and allow the steam to work for 5-10 minutes.
• For a fragrant aroma, place essential oils on a small plate near the shower, away from direct contact with the water.

Hand-washing

• While showering, fill the basin with water. You can also save bathwater or collect water in the sink.
• Add some eco-friendly detergent and work up a lather with your hands.
• Wash, rinse, and dry the item by hand.

Spot-cleaning

• Tackle fresh stains immediately with a sponge and lukewarm water. Even tricky stains like red wine can come off with enough scrubbing. Tip: pressing firmly on a wet stain absorbs and contains most of its moisture.
• When spot-cleaning at home, use detergent sparingly. Too much foam can create an unsightly ring around the cleaned area.

Brushing

Brushing works well for wools and tweeds because the fabric is closely knit, and stains tend to sit on the surface rather than penetrate deeply. A good brushing often removes dirt, jam, or even honey. After the gunk dries, use a natural bristle clothes brush to buff the stain away.

How we wash our clothes impacts the environment, so developing better laundry habits is crucial.

Even something as mundane as changing how we wash our clothes is an easy adjustment that we can make now. Understanding fabrics and their composition is critical to making our garments last longer.

chapters.title: Kiss your clothes goodbye
chapters.text:
Due to our modern attitude toward disposability, we must evaluate our waste and consider our apparel's lifespan and practical use as much as possible to make it last longer. This mindset isn't just about individuals; we're all responsible for restoring and reusing our items as a society. Local governments should provide adequate recycling facilities and promote local infrastructure to reduce clothing waste, such as mending. Additionally, people can take action by buying sensibly, caring for their clothes, and engaging in activities such as swapping and renting.

Instead of rearranging our clothes to fit better in the wardrobe, we should carefully select the ones we need and responsibly discard the rest.

This approach is crucial, especially considering one of the worst cultural tragedies we've experienced: the replacement of indigenous materials and costumes with cheap, disposable fashion. When we export discarded clothes to developing countries, we unknowingly wreak havoc on local economies and infrastructures. These exports also contribute to the decline of local artisanal industries, which suffer due to cheaply priced items and the perception that Western clothes are more fashionable than traditional clothing.

Giving away our excess isn't the solution we might think it is. Many receiving countries cannot handle the sheer volume of donations. Sadly, the harsh reality is that there's simply no room in our wardrobes or the world for all this unwanted clothing. Instead of thoughtlessly trashing or donating, we should consider more responsible options, such as recycling, repairing, reselling, or exchanging garments wisely first.

Ask yourself these questions to make better decisions about your clothes:

• Does it fit well?
• Is it in good condition? If so, can it be sold?
• Is it damaged? If yes, consider the next question.
• How can I reuse or recycle an item instead of discarding it?
• Could it be repurposed?

chapters.title: Make shopping a necessity, not a hobby
chapters.text:
We must restore the broken processes that led to our damaged garments. Since companies care about our purchasing habits, we have significant power to influence the systems that govern our consumption. It's crucial to understand the fashion industry, why its current model is flawed, and how we can get involved in pushing for improvements. That is how we can take full ownership of our purchases.

Finally, it is our responsibility to take care of the things we own (…) ~ Orsola de Castro

Technology has streamlined transactions and opened previously unimagined possibilities for globalizing the second-hand market. It used to be very difficult to share, repair, and purchase second-hand items. But today, there are creative online projects, apps, and platforms to help optimize our wardrobes and reduce wasteful spending. Buying and selling can now happen with just a click.

While this is great and game-changing for the industry, it's impractical to expect the rise of the second-hand market to solve the issue on its own without systemic changes. Keeping and preserving our clothes for as long as it takes is still the most effective option for reducing mass manufacturing.

If people purchase less, companies will have to produce less.

You can start by evaluating your clothes’ carbon footprint (using apps like Capture and For Good) and then check how transparent your favorite companies are. Try categorizing your clothing and rotating pieces to keep things fresh. Remember, we never truly get rid of anything we throw away; it just moves from our closets to the earth. Using worn clothing reduces our urge to buy new ones, and buying fewer clothes encourages us to do so more ethically, responsibly, and consciously.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Our clothing choices impact more than our style; they affect the environment and contribute to climate change. Many of us change outfits frequently without considering disposal or care, rarely repairing or altering clothes. We often have more than we need, failing to appreciate their beauty.

This insatiable desire for new clothes fuels a market rife with environmental and labor issues, tragically demonstrated by Bangladesh's 2013 Rana Plaza building collapse. To address these challenges, we must understand the importance of curbing excessive purchases and learn responsible methods for discarding, repairing, and reusing textiles.
